coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and visual imaging detection of dopamine based on carbon dots/copper nanoclusters dual-emitting nanohybrids
coredata.dc:creator: He, Weijie
Gui, Rijun
Jin, Hui
Wang, Benqian
Bu, Xiangning
Fu, Yongxin
coredata.dc:description: In this article, blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and NH4HCO3, and then combined with 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) to prepare APBA modified-CDs. APBA acted as the receptor of dopamine (DA). Using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a stabilizer and N2H4·H2O as a reducing reagent, BSA-stabilized and red-emitting copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) were prepared. By carbodiimide-activated coupling, novel nanohybrids consisting of CDs and CuNCs were constructed and exhibited dual-emitting fluorescence (FL). In the presence of DA, marked FL (at 440nm) quenching of nanohybrids was detected. Specific coupling interactions between boric acid of APBA and cis-glycol of DA induced the combination of DA and APBA on the surface of CDs. As a superior electron receptor, DA triggered the electron transfer from CDs to DA, resulting in the FL quenching of CDs in nanohybrids. The FL (at 640nm) of CuNCs in nanohybrids was almost unchanged after the addition of DA, and so further used for a reference FL to develop a novel ratiometric FL probe for DA detection. In addition to high sensitivity and selectivity, superior analytical performances of this probe were confirmed in applications, including dual-signal FL sensing of DA and naked-eye visual FL imaging of DA in aqueous solution and on filter paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper nanoclusters
Fluorescence
Ratiometric
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric FL probe
Assays of DA detection in different modes
Absorption and FL emission spectrum properties
CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids-based ratiometric FL probe of DA
Selectivity and sensitivity evaluation of the DA probe
Ratiometric FL and visual imaging detection of DA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong , China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong , China ( ZR2014BQ001 and ZR2016BB37 ), and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao (17-1-1-72-jch).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the as-prepared BSA-CuNCs (a), APBA-CDs (b) and CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (c).
FT-IR spectra of BSA-CuNCs (a), APBA-CDs (b) and CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (c).

UV–vis absorption spectra (black curves) and FL emission spectra (blue curves) of blue-emitting APBA-CDs (A), red-emitting BSA-CuNCs (B), and dual-emitting CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (C). Photographs of the corresponding products under daylight (left) and excitation with a UV lamp at 365nm (right) were shown in the insets. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

FL emission spectra and FL photographs of CDs/CuNCs (A), APBA-CDs (B) and BSA-CuNCs (C) after the addition of DA with a concentration varying from 0.1, 5, 10, 20, 35, 50, 80, 90, to 100μM (a-i). Inserted photographs were obtained upon excitation with a UV lamp at 365nm. (D) Linear plotting relationship between log(I640/I440) and C DA.
Ratiometric FL peak intensities (I640/I440) of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids after the addition of DA, OB and HRP (20μM), and other biological molecules with a concentration of 0.2mM.

FL images of the aqueous suspensions of sample-nanohybrid mixtures after the addition of DA with a concentration varying from 0.1 to 100μM. The mixtures were placed under room light and excited with a UV lamp at 365nm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and application procedures of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids-based ratiometric FL probe of DA.
A brief summary of different nanomaterials-based FL probes for the detection of DA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviation: polypyrrole (PPy); tyrosinase (TYR); hydroxy(mesityl)(pyren-l-yl)borane (HMPB); red-emission CdTe QDs (r-QDs); polydopamine (PDA); C3N4 nanosheets (C3N4NSs); 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of DA in real human serum samples based on this as-constructed ratiometric FL probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Samples were prepared by 50-fold diluting real human serum samples with PBS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Detected results were expressed as the average of six repeated measurements ± standard deviation (SD).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Relative standard deviation (RSD) results were defined as (SD/mean) × 100%.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and visual imaging detection of dopamine based on carbon dots/copper nanoclusters dual-emitting nanohybrids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textiles, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Laboratory of Fiber Materials and Modern Textiles, The Growing Base for State Key Laboratory, Qingdao University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and NH4HCO3, and then combined with 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) to prepare APBA modified-CDs. APBA acted as the receptor of dopamine (DA). Using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a stabilizer and N2H4·H2O as a reducing reagent, BSA-stabilized and red-emitting copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) were prepared. By carbodiimide-activated coupling, novel nanohybrids consisting of CDs and CuNCs were constructed and exhibited dual-emitting fluorescence (FL). In the presence of DA, marked FL (at 440nm) quenching of nanohybrids was detected. Specific coupling interactions between boric acid of APBA and cis-glycol of DA induced the combination of DA and APBA on the surface of CDs. As a superior electron receptor, DA triggered the electron transfer from CDs to DA, resulting in the FL quenching of CDs in nanohybrids. The FL (at 640nm) of CuNCs in nanohybrids was almost unchanged after the addition of DA, and so further used for a reference FL to develop a novel ratiometric FL probe for DA detection. In addition to high sensitivity and selectivity, superior analytical performances of this probe were confirmed in applications, including dual-signal FL sensing of DA and naked-eye visual FL imaging of DA in aqueous solution and on filter paper.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of significant neurotransmitters in human body and brain, dopamine (DA) plays a critical role on immune system and central nervous system connected with various diseases, such as schizophrenia, depression and Parkinson's disease [1,2]. A precise and sensitive detection of DA is in urgent demand for the diagnosis of neurological diseases. Currently, the conventional methods to determine DA mainly refer to electrochemistry [3], chromatography [4], enzymatic methods [5], etc. However, most of these methods require expensive instruments, laborious and time-consuming procedures, sometimes together with fluctuant measured results. These disadvantages probably restrain the conventional methods of DA detection in wide and practical applications. By contrast, the fluorimetric methods have more appealing advantages, including simple instrument, facile manipulation, high analysis sensitivity and selectivity [6–8]. Liu et al. developed a fluorescence (FL) probe for the detection of DA, based on water-soluble CuInS2 ternary quantum dots (QDs) [9]. Zhou et al. synthesized polypyrrole/graphene QDs core/shell hybrids that were used for a FL probe of DA [10]. Previous FL probes enabled the detection of DA, but single-signal FL sensing modes were still inferior. In real sample analysis, it is hard to acquire exact FL intensities due to FL fluctuation in reagent concentration and intrinsic background FL [11].

To overcome the defects of single-signal FL sensing, it is rational to construct ratiometric dual-signal FL. The development of ratiometric FL probes for DA detection is significant. The signal of ratiometric FL is independent of probe concentration and avoids the influence of environmental and intrinsic FL. Owing to self-calibration function of FL intensities, ratiometric FL probes have an improved sensitivity and accuracy [12,13]. Especially, ratiometric FL probes have a capability of detecting analytes based on analyte-triggered changes of FL dual-emissive colors that can be used to directly quantify analytes with naked eyes [14,15]. Ratiometric FL probes of DA were reported. Teng et al. developed a ratiometric FL probe of DA, using thiolate-protected gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) [16]. The reaction of tyrosinase and DA gave rise to a new emission band that was different from AuNCs. The new emission band and DA- triggered emission quenching of AuNCs could form a ratiometric FL probe of DA. Zhao et al. prepared water-insoluble hydroxy(mesityl)(pyren-l-yl)borane (HMPB) with two FL (monomer, excimer) bands [17]. When DA reacted with HMPB to generate a new compound, a reduced FL (peaked at 484nm) and an increased FL (peaked at 388nm) were detected, which could be used for developing a ratiometric FL probe of DA. Xu et al. designed a dual-QDs system containing CdTe QDs and carbon QDs [18]. This system enabled ratiometric FL detection of DA. Yu et al. synthesized a self-polymerized polydopamine that was utilized for ratiometric FL detection of DA [19].

Previous studies have mentioned ratiometric FL detection of DA [16–19], but the use of organic and biological molecules with low FL efficiency or toxic metal-containing semiconductor QDs to fabricate FL probes of DA probably limits the applications of probes, especially in real biological samples. Thus, a rational design of DA probes should conquer these limitations. Compared with semiconductor QDs containing heavy metals, carbon dots (CDs) have ultralow toxicity and high biocompatibility, suitable for biomedical applications [20]. Like CDs, fluorescent copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have lower toxic, higher biocompatibility and more environmental friendliness than semiconductor QDs [21]. Copper is much low-cost in comparison with other noble metals (gold, silver or platinum). CuNCs have become one of the most suitable candidates for biological analysis applications. Integration of fluorescent CDs and CuNCs to design novel dual-emitting CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids is significant and can be developed towards ratiometric FL sensing and visual FL imaging.

Herein, CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids were synthesized through carbodiimide-activated coupling between blue-emitting CDs and red-emitting CuNCs. In detail, CDs were combined with 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA). APBA served as the receptor of DA due to specific coupling interactions between boric acid groups of APBA and cis-glycol groups of DA. CuNCs were prepared in the matrix of bovine serum albumin (BSA) to improve the stability and biocompatibility of CuNCs. The addition of DA resulted in the formation of DA-APBA complex. Due to the approaching of DA to CDs in nanohybrids, DA as the electron receptor caused FL quenching of CDs based on a potential electron transfer from CDs to DA [22]. However, FL of CuNCs stabilized in the matrix of BSA was hardly any influenced and could serve as a reference FL. The dual-emitting CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids could be used for a novel ratiometric FL probe for DA detection ( Scheme 1). Based upon DA-triggered changes of FL dual-emissive colors, this probe could realize dual-signal FL sensing of DA and naked-eye visual FL imaging of DA in aqueous solution and on filter paper.

Sodium citrate, NH4HCO3, CuCl2·2H2O and N2H4·H2O (50wt% in water) were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. APBA, N-hydroxysuccinimide sodium (NHS), 1-ethyl-(3-(3-dimethylamino)propyl) carboxylate hydrochloride (EDC), BSA (M w = 68,000), ovalbumin (OB), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), glutathione (GSH), lysozyme (Lyz), norepinephrine (NE), threonine (Thr), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), cytochrome C (Cyt), phenylalanine (Phe), glutamic acid (Glu), DA and other biological reagents were bought from Aladdin. Other chemical reagents have the analytical grade and all reagents can be used directly as received without further purification. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was prepared through properly mixing Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. Double distilled water (DDW) was used in experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with a JEM-1400 TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan) accompanied by an operating acceleration voltage of 120kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Nicolet (USA) 5700 FTIR spectrometer with a KBr window in the transmission mode. FL excitation and emission spectra were measured with a FLSP 920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, U.K.), assembling with a Xenon lamp used for excitation. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Visual FL imaging of DA in aqueous solution and on filter paper was performed in the dark box equipped with a UV lamp for excitation at 365nm.

Blue-emissive CDs were prepared from a facile hydrothermal method [23]. Briefly, 0.1g of sodium citrate and 0.75g of NH4HCO3 were dissolved in 10mL of DDW under stirring. The resulting mixture was sealed into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 180°C for 4h. After that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution was purified by dialysis in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 1kDa) for ~ 24h in the dark, against DDW frequently. After dialysis, the solution in dialysis bag was evaporated to remove water and precipitated with acetone. The precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed with DDW, followed by drying treatment at 60°C to obtain dried powder of CDs. APBA-CDs were prepared based on a reported method [21]. In detail, 20mg of EDC and 10mg of NHS were dissolved in 20mL of DDW, followed by the addition of APBA (20mg). After stirring for 1h, the aqueous suspension of CDs (1.0mgmL−1) was added into the above mixture, together with an additional stirring for 2h at room temperature. The reaction solution was centrifuged to remove uncombined APBA. Supernatants were precipitated with acetone, separated by centrifugation and washed with DDW. The APBA-CDs products were dried and dispersed in DDW for subsequent experiments.

Red-emitting CuNCs were prepared through using BSA as a stabilizer and N2H4·H2O as a reducing reagent [21]. Typically, 0.1M of CuCl2 (0.25mL) and 5mgmL−1 of BSA (20mL) were mixed and stirred for 10min at room temperature. After that, a white hydrogel was formed due to the coordination between Cu2+ ions and functional groups of BSA (-NH, -SH, or -OH). After that, 0.5mL of N2H4·H2O (50wt% in water) was injected into the above mixture that was stirred for 5h. The color of mixture changed to light yellow, implying the formation of CuNCs. The reaction solution was precipitated with acetone to obtain products of BSA-CuNCs. The products were separated by centrifugation, washed with DDW, dried at 60°C, and dispersed in DDW for further use.

To prepare dual-emitting CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids, 15mg of EDC and 9mg of NHS were dissolved in 5mL of PBS (1mM, pH7.4). Under stirring, 10mg of dried BSA-CuNCs was added into the above mixture, keeping for a continuous reaction for 30min at room temperature. Then, 5mL of the aqueous suspension of CDs (1.0mgmL−1) was added and the reaction was performed for an additional 8h under stirring. Afterward, the reaction solution was precipitated by acetone. The precipitates of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids were separated by centrifugation, washed with DDW, treated by drying, and then dispersed in DDW for subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric FL probe
ce:section.ce:para: In 50mL of PBS (1mM, pH7.4) containing CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (0.1mgmL−1), DA was added under stirring to prepare a series of mixture solutions. The final concentration of DA (C DA) was adjusted to 0.1–100μM. FL emission spectra of different mixture solutions were respectively measured under excitation at 350nm. FL intensities peaked at ~440nm (from CDs, I440) and peaked at ~640nm (from CuNCs, I640) were converted into Log (I640/I440). The relationship between Log (I640/I440) and C DA was plotted linearly to fabricate the ratiometric FL probe of DA.

FL emission spectra of PBS (1mM, pH7.4) aqueous suspension of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (0.1mgmL−1) were measured in the presence of DA (20μM) or other biological molecules (GSH, Lyz, NE, Thr, Gly, His, Cyt, Phe, or Glu) with a concentration of 0.2mM. FL emission spectra of mixture solutions containing different molecules were measured to calculate ratiometric (I640/I440) values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assays of DA detection in different modes
ce:section.ce:para: In the PBS (1mM, pH7.4) aqueous suspension of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (0.1mgmL−1), DA was added to prepare mixture solutions, where the final C DA was adjusted to 0.1–100μM. Different mixture solutions in centrifuge tubes were transferred into a dark box and excited with a UV lamp at 365nm. A digital camera was used to record FL imaging. A tailored filter paper (5mm × 10mm) was immersed in each one of different mixture solutions and then placed on a clean slide. The FL imaging of filter papers loaded different mixture solutions was recorded in a dark box by using an equipped UV lamp, excited at 365nm. Under the same conditions except for DA replaced by DA-containing samples, human serum samples from a healthy young volunteer were diluted by 10-fold with PBS. The final C DA in different sample-nanohybrid systems was fixed to 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 50, or 100μM. FL spectra of DA-spiked systems were measured after incubation for 5min in the dark. The results of C DA were calculated by the plotted linear relationship between C DA and Log (I640/I440).

Red-emitting BSA-CuNCs were synthesized by a solution method at room temperature. N2H4·H2O was used as a reducing reagent. According to numerous carboxyl groups on the surface, BSA served as a stabilizer to synthesize CuNCs [21]. Blue-emissive CDs were prepared from hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate in the presence of NH4HCO3 [23]. CDs were conjugated with amine groups of APBA to prepare APBA-CDs. APBA acted as the receptor of DA because of the specific coupling interactions between boric acid groups of APBA and cis-glycol groups of DA. CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids with dual- emitting FL were prepared via a conventional carbodiimide-activated coupling between BSA-CuNCs and APBA-CDs [24]. The morphology and structure of products were characterized. In Fig. 1a, these observed monodisperse nanoparticles (NPs) from BSA-CuNCs exhibited a narrow diameter distribution of 1.8–2.5nm, with an average diameter of ca. 2.0nm. Fig. 1b illustrated that the average diameter of APBA-CDs was approximately 2.7nm. TEM images of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids (Fig. 1c) presented a conjugated structure of NPs. Based on preparation procedures, the conjugated structural NPs should be attributed to the coupling between BSA-CuNCs and APBA-CDs.

BSA-CuNCs, APBA-CDs and CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids were characterized by FT-IR spectra. In Fig. 2, two characteristic absorption bands located at 1345 and 1120cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration modes of B-O and C-B, respectively [21], indicating the presence of boronic acid groups in APBA-CDs and CDs/CuNCs. The -COOH and -NH2 groups on the CDs surface could be indicated by the characteristic bands at 3450, 1690 and 2830cm−1, in accordance with the vibration modes of C-OH, C˭O and N–H bonds [23]. The vibration peaks of -CONH groups located at ~ 1650cm−1 implied that -COOH groups from the surface of BSA-CuNCs had been combined with APBA of APBA-CDs. These results suggested the achievement of as-prepared products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and FL emission spectrum properties
ce:section.ce:para: The spectrum properties of products were researched. A characteristic absorption peak was located at ~ 340nm, as shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of APBA-CDs ( Fig. 3A). Upon excitation at the optimal wavelength of 340nm, there was an obvious FL emission centered at 440nm. APBA-CDs had a bright blue-emitting FL under 365nm of excitation with UV lamp (inserts in Fig. 3A). In Fig. 3B, BSA-CuNCs exhibited an intensive FL emission with a sharp FL peaked at ~640nm. Red-emitting FL under 365nm of excitation with UV lamp was observed in the inserts of Fig. 3B. As for CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids, an improved characteristic absorption peak around 240nm and a marked absorption peak located at 340nm could be observed (Fig. 3C). This result was attributed to the coupling of APBA-CDs with BSA-CuNCs, inducing the integration of characteristic absorption bands. FL emission spectrum of CDs/CuNCs showed two distinct FL peaks at 440 and 640nm, which are assigned to the characteristic FL emission peaks from APBA-CDs and BSA-CuNCs, respectively. CDs/CuNCs showed a light blue- emitting FL under 365nm of excitation with UV lamp (inserts in Fig. 3C), which was mainly due to a much higher emission intensity of APBA-CDs than BSA-CuNCs in nanohybrids. When FL intensities of BSA-CuNCs were retained almost unchanged, the regular decrease of FL intensities of APBA-CDs would trigger a gradual change of FL emission colors from blue to red [25]. The unique FL emission properties of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids could be further developed towards ratiometric FL sensing and visual FL imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids-based ratiometric FL probe of DA
ce:section.ce:para: In the aqueous suspension of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids containing DA, FL emission spectra showed a regular decrease in FL intensities peaked at 440nm when the coexisting concentration of DA (C DA) varied from 0.1 to 100μM. However, FL peak intensities at 640nm were almost unchanged during the addition of DA ( Fig. 4A). FL emission peak intensity changes at 440nm were assigned to DA-induced FL quenching of APBA-CDs in nanohybrids. The specific coupling of APBA with DA could induce the electron transfer from CDs to DA (as an electron acceptor) [22]. As confirmed, APBA-CDs served as a reference of CDs/CuNCs (Fig. 4B). BSA-CuNCs were stabilized in the BSA matrix and departed from DA, and thus FL emission of CuNCs was only influenced negligibly (Fig. 4C). FL emission responses of products on C DA could be also observed by naked eyes. As inserted in Fig. 4A, FL emission colors of CDs/CuNCs gradually changed from blue to red during the addition of DA (0.1–100μM), in accordance with FL emission spectrum changes (FL quenching peaked at 440nm, almost unchanged FL peaked at 640nm). Moreover, DA-induced FL quenching process of APBA-CDs was visual based on the change of FL colors from deep-blue to light-blue (insets in Fig. 4B). Under the same conditions, FL of BSA- CuNCs did not exhibit obvious changes both in FL emission color and intensity. As inserted in Fig. 4C, negligible changes in the red-emitting FL color and intensity were detected after the addition of DA at different coexisting concentrations.

DA-dependent FL intensities and color changes of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids indicated the potential for ratiometric FL sensing and visual FL imaging of DA. The nanohybrids could be further developed as a novel sensing system (FL probe) for efficient detection of DA. To construct a novel ratiometric FL probe of DA based on CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids, the relationship between ratiometric FL peak intensity and C DA was investigated. In Fig. 4D, there was a well plotted linear relationship between Log (I640/I440) and C DA varying from 0.1 to 100μM. The linear regression equation was expressed as Log (I640/I440) = 0.00457 C DA – 0.12576 (R 2 = 0.9990). The limit of detection (LOD) was appropriately 32nM, calculated by 3σ/k (“σ” is the standard deviation from six repeated detections of blank solution, “k” is the slope of calibration curve) [24]. Thus, CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids could realize the ratiometric FL sensing (Fig. 4D) and FL visual imaging (Fig. 4A-C) of DA. As summarized in Table 1, the LOD and linear detection range of DA from this as-constructed FL probe were comparable and superior to previous researches, where other nanomaterilas were used to develop FL probes of DA [8–10,16–19,26–29]. Moreover, the integration of biocompatible FL-emissive CDs and CuNCs to design novel dual-emitting CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids was superior to the substances in other probe systems for DA detection. Experimental results (in Fig. 4) revealed the feasibility of ratiometric FL sensing and visual FL imaging of DA. These results suggested the superiority of this as-constructed FL probe for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity evaluation of the DA probe
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selective and sensitive ratiometric FL responses of this FL probe, some molecules probably existing in real human serum samples were selected as the interferents of DA, including GSH, Lyz, NE, Thr, Gly, His, Cyt, Phe and Glu. As a result (in Fig. 5), 20μM of DA triggered a dramatic increase (~ 1.25) in ratiometric FL peak intensities (I640/I440). By contrast, 0.2mM of each interferent only resulted in slight changes (~ 0.15) in I640/I440. Each interferent even at a coexisting concentration as 10-fold higher as C DA was not enough to generate a remarkable influence on I640/I440. In other words, the ratiometric FL responses of this probe were highly selective and sensitive towards DA, over other relative molecules. In the presence of each interferent (20μM), the detected C DA was very close to the given one (20μM) with the maximum error of less than 5.0%. In addition, some biological molecules containing diols groups such as glycoproteins (OB, HRP) also induced a marked FL change in I640/I440 when compared to other molecules. OB or HRP has the same characteristic molecular structure as DA and acts as an efficient electron acceptor to trigger the electron transfer from CDs to OB or HRP [21]. These results further revealed the specific coupling interactions between the boric acid groups of APBA and cis-glycol groups of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric FL and visual imaging detection of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of this ratiometric FL probe for DA detection, its analytical performance was examined in real human serum samples. Real human serum samples obtained from a healthy young volunteer were only 10-fold diluted with PBS, without further purification. In the aqueous suspension of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids, human serum samples containing DA were added to prepare a series of sample-nanohybrid systems. Based on the linear relationship between Log (I640/I440) and C DA in Fig. 4D, C DA in real samples could be achieved through measuring FL emission spectra of sample-nanohybrid systems. As summarized in Table 2, the detected values of C DA from this probe were in good agreement with spiked values, accompanied by high detection recoveries (98–104%), low RSD values and narrow RSD distribution (1.92–3.06%). In general, some relative biological molecules as mentioned in Fig. 5 may coexist with DA in real human serum samples. High detection recoveries and low RSD indicated that the presence of potential interferents hardly any affected on DA detection, verifying high feasibility of this probe for DA detection in real samples.

To realize visual FL imaging detection of DA, the aqueous suspensions of nanohybrids were placed in centrifuge tubes and irradiated with a UV lamp at 365nm of excitation. In Fig. 4A, regular FL color changes from deep-blue, light-blue, light-red to deep-red could be observable when the concentration of DA increased from 0.1 to 100μM. One of FL colors represented the corresponding C DA in the aqueous suspension of nanohybrids. FL color changes reflected the changes of DA concentrations. These results suggested the potential of CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids for visual FL imaging detection of DA [30–32]. Besides, visual FL imaging of DA was further studied in real samples. Different aqueous suspensions consisting of real human serum samples and nanohybrids were prepared as summarized in Table 2. A tailored filter paper (5mm × 10mm) was immersed in the each aqueous suspension containing DA at a fixed concentration, and placed on a clean slide for imaging experiments. Upon 365nm of excitation with a UV lamp, a series of FL images were obtained in Fig. 6. The each FL color was assigned to the corresponding C DA in the aqueous suspension of sample-nanohybrid mixtures. Based on the observed FL colors of sample-nanohybrid mixtures, the corresponding values of C DA could be obtained. These results revealed that this ratiometric FL probe enabled visual imaging of DA in real samples, indicating the capability of on-site and naked-eye visual detection of DA.

In summary, CDs/CuNCs nanohybrids were prepared through facile carbodiimide-activated coupling between blue-emitting APBA-CDs and red-emitting BSA-CuNCs. The nanohybrids combined with DA because of specific interactions between boric acid groups of APBA and cis-glycol groups of DA. The specific combination of APBA with DA resulted in the FL (peaked at 440nm) quenching of CDs in nanohybrids due to the electron transfer from CDs to DA. However, DA did not markedly influence on the FL (peaked at 640nm) of CuNCs. A well linear relationship (R 2 = 0.990) between Log (I640/I440) and C DA in the range of 0.1–100µM was plotted, together with a low LOD of 32nM. The ratiometric FL peak intensity responses of nanohybrids were highly selective and sensitive on DA, over interferents that may exist in real samples. The nanohybrids-based ratiometric FL probe enabled effective detection of DA in human serum samples, accompanied by high detection recoveries and low RSD. Moreover, this as-constructed ratiometric FL probe realized on-site and naked-eye visual FL imaging detection of DA in real sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong, China (ZR2014BQ001 and ZR2016BB37), and the Source Innovation Plan Application Basic Research Project of Qingdao (17-1-1-72-jch).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of dopamine using porphyrin-functionalized graphene
Biosens. Bioelectron.
